metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `analyzers: dict[str,str]` can be served by the existing flat-key coercion infrastructure, or reconsider as `tuple[str,...]` of `lang:tool` pairs (matching the established `_as_str_tuple` coercer) — and if a dict type is kept, specify the new coercer, the TOML form, and whether/how `rebar -c` overrides apply.
evidence: All 13 existing `_SECTIONS` entries use scalar or `_as_str_tuple` coercers; `grep 'dict\[str' src/rebar/_config_schema.py` returns no hits
No `_as_dict` or similar coercer exists anywhere in `_config_schema.py`
Env-override layer in `_config_sources.py:env_overrides()` iterates `_SECTIONS[sect]` flat keys — a dict-valued key would have no defined `REBAR_CODE_HEALTH_ANALYZERS` env-override shape
Zero existing call-sites for a dict-typed config value (Rule-of-Three: 0 < 3)
findings.finding: The `analyzers: dict[str,str]` field type has zero precedent in the existing `_SECTIONS` coercion table: every one of the 13 existing sections maps keys to scalar/tuple coercers — no coercer for a `dict[str,str]` sub-value exists. The plan specifies the AC type but not the coercion mechanism, env-override behavior (each lang->tool pair would need its own `REBAR_CODE_HEALTH_ANALYZERS_<LANG>` env var, or the whole dict would be un-overridable), or whether this is a TOML inline-table (`analyzers = {python = 'ruff'}`) or dotted-key form — introducing a structurally new coercer type to serve a single section risks over-engineering.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `analyzers: dict[str,str]` field; src/rebar/_config_schema.py `_SECTIONS` coercion table
scenarios: Author writes the `_SECTIONS['code_health']['analyzers']` coercer entry — there is no existing helper to call, so either a bespoke lambda is added (new mechanism) or the key is left uncoerced (silently accepts any type).

A user tries `rebar -c code_health.analyzers.python=ruff` — the `parse_cli_overrides` function only splits on the FIRST dot (`sect, key = dotted.split('.', 1)`), so `code_health.analyzers.python=ruff` would set `analyzers.python` as the key under `code_health`, not a nested dict — the override is silently dropped or errors.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `analyzers: dict[str,str]` with `analyzers: tuple[str,...] = ()` (e.g. `"python:ruff,go:staticcheck"` comma-separated `lang:tool` pairs parsed by a small extension of `_as_str_tuple`). This stays within the established coercion vocabulary, avoids a new mechanism, and keeps env-override behavior well-defined. The consuming code (0734) can split each entry on `:` to recover the dict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `analyzers: dict[str,str]` is coerced in `_SECTIONS` (e.g. add an `_as_str_dict` helper that validates a TOML sub-table is a flat `{str: str}` mapping, and document that `_emit_toml` does not support writing this field — or change the type to a `tuple[str,...]` of `lang:tool` pairs to reuse `_as_str_tuple`).
evidence: AC: '`analyzers: dict[str,str]` (lang->tool)'

_config_schema.py coerce_sparse loop: `for key, coercer in _SECTIONS[sect].items(): if key in d: coerced[key] = coercer(d.pop(key), ...)`  — a key with no coercer entry is left in `d` and falls through to `_warn_unknown`
config.py `_emit_toml`: raises ConfigError on `isinstance(value, dict)` for any section key
ABSENCE: no `_as_str_dict` or equivalent helper exists anywhere in _config_schema.py
findings.finding: The `analyzers: dict[str,str]` field type has no corresponding coercer in the `_SECTIONS` mechanism: every existing field uses a scalar coercer (`_as_bool`, `_as_str`, `_as_str_tuple`, etc.), none handle a dict value. Without a registered coercer, `coerce_sparse` will treat `analyzers` as an unknown key and warn-drop it (or log a warning in strict mode). Additionally, `_emit_toml` explicitly raises `ConfigError` for dict-valued section keys, so any config round-trip write (e.g. via `write_jira_config`-style tooling) that touches a `[code_health]` section with a populated `analyzers` sub-table would error. The plan specifies the field type but leaves the coercion path entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `analyzers: dict[str,str]` field; _config_schema.py `_SECTIONS` coercer table
scenarios: Implementer adds `analyzers` to the dataclass but omits a `_SECTIONS` coercer entry → any TOML `[code_health] analyzers = {python = 'ruff'}` is silently dropped with a warning, config reads empty dict
Implementer adds a pass-through `lambda v, k: v` coercer → `_emit_toml` round-trips break for any tool that writes the config file
Strict mode (`REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error`) → a populated `analyzers` sub-table raises ConfigError at load
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a `_as_str_dict(v, key)` coercer that validates `v` is a `dict` with all `str` keys and values and raises `ConfigError` otherwise, register it in `_SECTIONS['code_health']['analyzers']`, and note the `_emit_toml` limitation; or (b) change the field type to `tuple[str, ...]` of `"lang:tool"` tokens (e.g. `("python:ruff", "go:staticcheck")`) and use `_as_str_tuple`, consistent with every other multi-value field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC and at least one Verification test case covering invalid-type and out-of-range inputs for `size_cap` and `size_near_fraction` (e.g., non-integer `size_cap`, `size_near_fraction` outside [0,1]) — specify whether rebar raises a typed validation error or silently clamps/rejects.
evidence: 'rebar -c code_health.<key>=<val> overrides parse via the existing path'
'Unit: default load, -c override parse, missing-section default. Golden surface test passes.'
size_near_fraction: float has an implicit valid range [0,1] not enforced by a generic override path
No AC or Verification item covers invalid-type or out-of-range rejection/warning behavior
findings.finding: The plan introduces a user-facing config surface (`rebar -c code_health.<key>=<val>`) but only specifies behavior for the happy path (valid values parsed) and the missing-section default. It does not specify what happens when a user supplies an invalid type (e.g., a string for `size_cap`) or an out-of-range value (e.g., `size_near_fraction=1.5` or `-0.2`). The Verification AC lists only 'default load, -c override parse, missing-section default' — no test for malformed or out-of-range input. The existing override path is generic and cannot enforce field-specific range constraints on `size_near_fraction` without explicit schema-level validation logic.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'Defaults documented; `rebar -c code_health.<key>=<val>` overrides parse via the existing path' and Verification AC
scenarios: A user runs `rebar -c code_health.size_near_fraction=2.5` — no AC or test specifies whether this silently accepts 2.5, clamps it, or raises an error, leaving UX undefined.
A user runs `rebar -c code_health.size_cap=notanumber` — undefined whether the CLI surfaces a typed error message or crashes with an unhandled exception.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'Invalid types (e.g., non-integer size_cap) raise a ConfigValidationError with a human-readable message; size_near_fraction outside [0,1] raises ConfigValidationError.' Add corresponding test cases to tests/unit/test_code_health_config.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add both a `code_health: CodeHealthConfig = field(default_factory=CodeHealthConfig)` field to the `Config` dataclass AND a `'code_health': CodeHealthConfig` entry to `_SECTION_CLASSES` in `_config_schema.py` (required by `Config.from_mapping`'s `_SECTION_CLASSES[sect]` dispatch — omitting either causes a runtime KeyError/TypeError on any `[code_health]` config input).
evidence: Config.from_mapping at _config_schema.py:549: `return cls(**{sect: _SECTION_CLASSES[sect](**vals) for sect, vals in sparse.items()})` — both _SECTION_CLASSES and a Config field are required
_SECTION_CLASSES dict at lines 553–567 lists every section explicitly; no auto-registration
Config dataclass at lines 527–539 lists every section as an explicit field; no auto-field
Files Likely Touched lists only _config_schema.py, config.py, config_surface.json, test_code_health_config.py — does not call out the _SECTION_CLASSES entry or Config field as distinct steps
ABSENCE: neither 'SECTION_CLASSES' nor 'Config field' nor 'code_health field' appears anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The plan asserts '`rebar -c code_health.<key>=<val>` overrides parse via the existing path' but two required wiring points are absent from the AC and Files Likely Touched: (a) a `code_health: CodeHealthConfig` field must be added to the `Config` dataclass (lines 527–539 of _config_schema.py), and (b) `'code_health': CodeHealthConfig` must be added to `_SECTION_CLASSES` (lines 553–567). Without both, `Config.from_mapping` (line 549: `cls(**{sect: _SECTION_CLASSES[sect](**vals) ...})`) will raise `KeyError` on a `code_health` override even if `_SECTIONS` and the dataclass exist.
findings.location: AC: '`rebar -c code_health.<key>=<val>` overrides parse via the existing path; section re-exported through config.py' / src/rebar/_config_schema.py lines 522–567
scenarios: Developer adds CodeHealthConfig dataclass and _SECTIONS entry but misses _SECTION_CLASSES → Config.from_mapping raises KeyError on any rebar.toml with a [code_health] section
Developer adds _SECTION_CLASSES but omits the Config dataclass field → Config(**{... 'code_health': CodeHealthConfig(...)}) raises TypeError for unexpected keyword argument
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: '`Config` dataclass gains a `code_health: CodeHealthConfig = field(default_factory=CodeHealthConfig)` field and `_SECTION_CLASSES` gains `"code_health": CodeHealthConfig`.' Add both to Files Likely Touched as explicit required edits.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a default value for `scan_roots` in the AC field spec (e.g. `scan_roots: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)`) so `CodeHealthConfig()` is valid when the section is absent from TOML.
evidence: AC line: 'scan_roots: list[str]' — no default value given
All other AC fields carry defaults: 'enabled: bool=False', 'size_cap: int|None', 'size_near_fraction: float'
Existing pattern in _config_schema.py: every dataclass field used in Config.from_mapping (e.g. VerifyConfig, SyncConfig) has a default so the section can be omitted from TOML
Verification AC: 'missing-section default' must work — impossible with a required positional field
findings.finding: scan_roots: list[str] is declared in the AC without a default value; every other field in the dataclass spec has an explicit default (enabled=False, size_cap=int|None, size_near_fraction=float). A bare list[str] field with no default in a dataclass that is instantiated as CodeHealthConfig() in Config.from_mapping would raise TypeError at runtime, and the missing-section-default Verification scenario would fail.
scenarios: A user omits [code_health] from their rebar.toml — Config.from_mapping calls CodeHealthConfig() which raises TypeError because scan_roots has no default.
The unit test 'missing-section default' case in the Verification AC fails at instantiation time.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC field to: `scan_roots: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)` (empty list = no roots configured, consistent with `enabled=False` default).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change `scan_roots: list[str]` to `scan_roots: tuple[str, ...] = ()` in the AC to match the schema's uniform multi-value string convention (and reuse `_as_str_tuple` directly with no new coercer).
evidence: `completion_preserve_criteria: tuple[str, ...] = (...)`, `http_allowed_hosts: tuple[str, ...] = ()`, `http_allowed_origins: tuple[str, ...] = ()`, `auth_strategies: tuple[str, ...] = ()`, `auth_required_scopes: tuple[str, ...] = ()` — all multi-value string fields are tuples
`_as_str_tuple` returns `tuple[x for x in items if x]` — it cannot return a `list`
Zero existing `list[str]` config fields (Rule-of-Three: 0 < 3)
findings.finding: The `scan_roots: list[str]` field type conflicts with the schema's established convention: every multi-value string field across all 13 existing sections uses `tuple[str, ...]` (coerced by `_as_str_tuple`), not `list[str]`. The plan specifies `list[str]` in the AC without noting whether a new `list`-returning coercer is needed or whether this is an intentional deviation from the tuple-everywhere pattern.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `scan_roots: list[str]` field; src/rebar/_config_schema.py existing multi-value fields
scenarios: The AC specifies `list[str]` but the existing `_as_str_tuple` coercer returns a `tuple`; either a new `_as_str_list` coercer is added (new surface) or the field is silently stored as a tuple despite the AC type annotation.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to `scan_roots: tuple[str, ...] = ()` — consistent with every other multi-value string field in the schema and directly served by the existing `_as_str_tuple` coercer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the AC for config.py explicit: add `from rebar._config_schema import CodeHealthConfig as CodeHealthConfig` to config.py's re-export block (the same pattern as VerifyConfig, McpConfig, etc.).
evidence: src/rebar/config.py lines 27-38 show every section class is individually re-exported: 'from rebar._config_schema import VerifyConfig as VerifyConfig', etc.
IdentityConfig — also defined in _config_schema.py — is conspicuously absent from config.py's explicit re-exports, showing this is a real omission risk.
AC says 're-exported through config.py' but does not specify the concrete line needed
findings.finding: config.py is listed in 'Files likely touched' but the plan's AC and description do not explicitly mention adding the `CodeHealthConfig as CodeHealthConfig` re-export line to config.py's explicit re-export block. Every existing section class (VerifyConfig, McpConfig, UiConfig, etc.) has an individual explicit re-export in config.py lines 27–38; omitting CodeHealthConfig from that block would leave `from rebar.config import CodeHealthConfig` broken despite AC saying 'section re-exported through config.py'.
findings.location: Files likely touched: src/rebar/config.py; AC line 'section re-exported through config.py'
scenarios: A downstream caller does `from rebar.config import CodeHealthConfig` (following the established pattern) and gets ImportError because the explicit re-export line was forgotten.
A future consumer in ticket 0734 imports CodeHealthConfig from rebar.config and discovers the import fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Add `from rebar._config_schema import CodeHealthConfig as CodeHealthConfig` to config.py alongside the existing per-section re-export lines (27–38).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change `scan_roots: list[str]` to `scan_roots: tuple[str, ...] = ()` to align with the established schema convention for multi-value string fields (all backed by `_as_str_tuple`).
evidence: AC: '`scan_roots: list[str]`'
_config_schema.py McpConfig: `http_allowed_hosts: tuple[str, ...] = ()`, `auth_strategies: tuple[str, ...] = ()`, etc.
_as_str_tuple docstring: 'A tuple of non-empty, trimmed strings'
ABSENCE: no field in the existing schema uses `list[str]` — all multi-value string fields are `tuple[str, ...]`
findings.finding: The plan specifies `scan_roots: list[str]` but every comparable multi-value string field in the schema (`http_allowed_hosts`, `auth_strategies`, `completion_preserve_criteria`, `auth_required_scopes`, etc.) uses `tuple[str, ...]` backed by `_as_str_tuple`, which returns a `tuple`. Annotating `scan_roots` as `list[str]` while the coercer returns a `tuple` creates a type annotation vs. runtime mismatch that `mypy`/`pyright` will flag and that could cause subtle bugs in code that appends to the list.
scenarios: Implementer annotates `scan_roots: list[str]` and the coercer returns a tuple → static type checkers report an error on the dataclass field assignment in `from_mapping`
Consumer code downstream (ticket 0734) calls `cfg.code_health.scan_roots.append(...)` expecting a list → AttributeError at runtime
findings.suggested_fix: Use `scan_roots: tuple[str, ...] = ()` and coerce with `lambda v, k: _as_str_tuple(v, k)`, exactly as `http_allowed_hosts` and `completion_preserve_criteria` do.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit behavioral assertion in `test_code_health_config.py` that independently checks each expected key, type, and default value (e.g., `assert config.code_health.enabled is False`, `assert isinstance(config.code_health.scan_roots, list)`, etc.) rather than relying solely on the regenerated golden snapshot to validate the new section's correctness.
evidence: Plan says 'regenerate + review it' in review-driven corrections but the AC only requires 'golden test green'

No independent expected-dict (e.g., inline fixture asserting specific keys `enabled`, `scan_roots`, `analyzers`, `size_cap`, `size_near_fraction` with their types/defaults) is required by any AC or verification step
The golden test file `test_config_surface_golden.py` by its nature compares code output to a file the author just generated from the same code
findings.finding: The golden-file workflow (regenerate `config_surface.json`, then assert test is green) is a self-authored-oracle / change-detector: it snapshots whatever the new code emits and asserts it matches, which locks in any bug in the schema definition without an independent expected-value check. The AC 'golden surface test passes' is fully satisfiable even if the emitted keys or types are wrong, as long as the regenerated file and the code agree.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — '`tests/golden/config_surface.json` regenerated to include the section; golden test green.' / Verification section
scenarios: Developer introduces a typo in a field name (e.g., `analyzerz`) or wrong default (e.g., `enabled=True`); regenerates golden; test goes green; bug ships undetected.
A type annotation is dropped; the dataclass still serialises; golden matches; no test catches the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_code_health_config.py`, add a test that constructs a `CodeHealthConfig` from defaults and asserts each concrete key by name and value (e.g., `cfg = CodeHealthConfig(); assert cfg.enabled is False; assert cfg.scan_roots == []; assert cfg.size_cap is None`). Keep the golden file for surface-drift detection, but make the unit test the authoritative correctness check.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
